design_philosophy.core_loop: Combat encounters interrupted by NPC dialogue, trading, and mission assignment; hub-based exploration with stealth options and conversation-driven progression
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing combat engagement against stealth infiltration; resolving moral ambiguity through player choice at story climax
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Reluctant mercenary becoming instrumental to a resistance movement; gaining agency through dialogue and choice rather than pure firepower
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hybrid FPS-RPG that prioritizes NPC interaction, stealth mechanics, and narrative consequence over run-and-gun gameplay; non-linear hub-based structure challenges FPS conventions; critical story choice (Oracle vs. Macil) branches narrative and affects ending

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player can approach encounters stealthily using dagger or poison bolts to avoid alarms, or engage directly; stealth path reduces enemy reinforcements and alarm escalation

progression.upgrade_system: Two numerical attributes: accuracy (ranged weapon effectiveness) and stamina (maximum health pool), improvable at designated points throughout game
progression.unlock_mechanism: Mission-based progression through NPC dialogue and assignment; story branching at critical choice points; Sigil fragment acquisition gates major narrative beats
replayability_hooks: Binary choice between trusting Oracle or Macil affects story branch and ending
Stealth vs. direct combat playstyle options
Multiple NPC interactions and trading paths
Hub-based world layout allows revisiting areas with different loadouts

world_building_techniques: NPC dialogue exposition regarding Order's religious control and plague origins
Environmental storytelling through castle, factory, and town layouts reflecting Order's infrastructure
Codename-based communication (Blackbird) providing real-time mission updates and commentary
Character-driven plot through rebel leaders (Macil, Rowan) and antagonists (The Programmer, The Bishop, The Oracle)
Lore delivery through Oracle's revelations about Sigil fragments and character motivations

boss_design.philosophy: Named antagonists serve as story gatekeepers requiring defeat to progress; bosses represent Order hierarchy (The Programmer, The Bishop); final choice involves potential betrayal rather than traditional final battle
notable_bosses: The Programmer - Order lieutenant wielding Sigil fragment; first major confrontation after escape
The Bishop - Order leader holding second Sigil fragment; direct encounter checkpoint
The Oracle - Reveals truth about Macil; player can kill or trust (narrative choice replaces boss fight)

faction_indicators: The Order - religious authoritarian organization controlling populace through fear and indoctrination
The Front - resistance movement led by Macil with operatives like Rowan and Blackbird
Mutated virus victims aligned with Order deity
danger_signals: Order guards enforce weapons restrictions and surveillance
Firing weapons outside combat triggers alarms and guard activation
Castle stronghold represents Order's power concentration
Betrayal potential within resistance (Macil as potential traitor)
environmental_storytelling: Town of Tarnhill as civilian hub under Order occupation
Castle architecture reflecting Order's militarized control
Factory structure indicating resource extraction for war machine
Scattered weapons and defensive positions showing ongoing conflict
